speaker: interviewee
text: Went into the house. They came back the next week to see us. And it-- and we went once there, but later on, you weren't allowed to go to non-Jewish people. But we went anyway, and we had a friendship between them. They were about our age, young people.

speaker: interviewer
text: She became-- she had the baby, and we celebrated that. And it became voiced and voiced, so we wanted to find a place. We didn't want to go away. So I said to my husband, you know, if we could hide by somebody, we don't have to go to Poland. You know? So one day, I went up there. She was alone. And I  ask her, would you mind taking us in to hide us? So she said, I can't make a decision like that. I will have to talk it over with my husband. I said, that's fine. A couple days later, he came to our store, and he told us that they have decided to take us.

speaker: interviewee
text: We went up there. It was December 5. Because this was a holiday-- it is like Santa Claus. And Holland celebrates that. That's why I remember the date, December 5. We went up there. We took along whatever we could carry, what wasn't too much. But before we went, we had a lot of leather. We gave him that, because that was valuable. Because you couldn't get that no more, rubber and leather. And we gave him along a little bit clothing, and we ourselves went a couple of times back and forth with clothing that we could carry. And for the rest, we left everything standing the way it was. The apartment, the machinery, everything we left. We couldn't take that. We locked the door, and that was the last time we saw any of our possessions. It was later, [NON-ENGLISH], what means that the Dutch people broke sometime in when they saw that the Jewish people were not there no more. They took everything out, or it was picked up from the Germans. I don't know. I have never gotten anything back from it. No-- no money, nor the-- nor the things themselves. We never got anything from Holland back. They told us that we were not citizen, so we couldn't get nothing. So we never got anything paid for.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of this couple?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jan and Lucie Kloek. They lived in Amsterdam, not far away from those. They lived in one room at the time. Then they got an apartment, and when we went hiding, we went to the apartment. The apartment was in-- in an apartment house where there was three floors. On each floor lived one family. And there lived on the top floor-- so we used to go to their house. And on top was an attic. So we slept in the attic. But in the daytime, when there was nobody, we went downstairs. So we [? stood ?] with her. She had the baby. We helped her, whatever we could, to stay in the house. And when somebody came, they had to ring the bell downstairs. By the time that they came up, we were gone. We went to the attic.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe what the attic was like? The size of it, what you had up there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Small room, with a very small window that we never opened. Only at night, because we were scared somebody would see us. We had in the attic one bed and a little bit of our possessions. But later on, when it became that the-- the German came, at the time, we took everything apart. We had nothing that showed that people lived there no more. We lived in the hiding place at night. I slept inside there. My husband was sleeping outside the hiding place. So when something would have happened, we both would have gone inside and closed the door. But we had nothing left no more, like a bed or a blanket or anything. You couldn't see that people lived there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the address?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes. I will never forget that. 380 [? Kapstadt, ?] in Amsterdam, [INAUDIBLE]. We were right near the Amstel, near the water. Two minutes away from the Jewish district, where later was the [NON-ENGLISH]. Where the [NON-ENGLISH] was. Amsterdam has a lot of water, and a lot of bridges. All the bridges around the Jewish district were pulled up, so Jewish could not go out no more. They were automatic locked inside there, where they were picked up. We heard the screaming, we heard the screaming, and we heard the commotion, what's going on. And we lived right a half a block away from it. We couldn't see anything, because we couldn't open the windows, but we listened to what was going on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me any incidents you remember of the SS coming, maybe?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, there were. My husband, I think, told you already. The SS came to the door. The woman was at the time in the hospital. She had a second baby, that little boy. The first one was given away to friends, because so-called nobody was in the house. So we couldn't take care of her, because we were alone. She was at the time in the hospital when they knocked on the door. He was at work. But we had underneath of us, on the second floor, a woman, who was a Catholic. And I think she knew what was going on, because when the man came home, she-- she called him. And she said to him, Mr. Kloek, if you have anything that is not right, get rid of them, get rid of it, because they said they're going to come tonight to you.

speaker: interviewer
text: He came upstairs. He was white like a sheet. He was frightened, and he told us that. So we said, what can we do? That's what-- when we went upstairs, and we took the bed, and everything away, that you shouldn't be able to see anything. And he said to us, you cannot stay here. If they're coming back, they're going to find you. So he went next door, and there was an open attic, and we went there. There was old coals laying, and it was cold. We had the coats on. We laid in the coals and in the dirt. We had no choice. And he said, if there's anything, I come. If nobody comes, I come in the morning to-- to talk with you.

speaker: interviewee
text: And indeed, he came in the morning. Nobody came back. And he said, but I can't keep you. If they're coming, not today, maybe they're going to come tomorrow. He said, the best way for you is to come to my work place. I have nowhere else to take you. And that's how we went to his work place, and we [? stood ?] there.

speaker: interviewer
text: I want to back up a minute. Can you remember what that was like for you when you heard the SS come into the apartment below?

speaker: interviewee
text: I started shivering, because I know if they will find us, that we will-- first of all, they will hit us to death. We will never make Auschwitz. We will be punished before we go. There was a name, [NON-ENGLISH], that was the German had there their headquarters, the SS. We would have been taken there, and I don't think we would have made it to Auschwitz. I was very frightened. We [? stood ?] outside. It was like a-- a clothes closet into the veranda, or so how you call that. And outside, we [? stood ?] in there. We shivered. We couldn't do nothing. We heard the SS with the boots coming up. And my husband said to-- he was laying on the couch. He make with the finger to me, don't move. And I didn't move. I was like paralyzed. And thank God that they didn't break in the door. Usually they would, with the boots, trample in the door. But I heard them running upstairs and looking by the attics, but all the attics were locked. They didn't know which one was ours or which were not. And then they went down, and talked with the woman.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that was the only time that we heard them. They never came back, thank God. But I think they were looking for somebody. They came special to that address to look for somebody. I don't know why just to do that. Probably they had a hunch that somebody was there. What year was it now that you went to live in the workshop?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember. I think it must have be '44 or something like that. We lived there for six weeks. And at the time, her mother came from Holligen to be with her when she had the baby. And naturally, she wasn't allowed to see us. And we lived in the attic-- in the workshop. And then, after six weeks, we couldn't stay in there no more. We had nothing, no facilities. We lived on a blanket on the floor. You know, it was dirty. It was in the red lights district. And we saw the German coming to the prostitutes. And so, it didn't interfere with us. We had nothing to do with that.

speaker: interviewer
text: But after six weeks, he said, what are we going to do? He said, you have to-- they didn't come so far. Come back, and we will see what we can do. Her mother was still here, so she wasn't allowed to see us.

speaker: interviewee
text: At the time, so long as her mother was in the house, we never came down. We always [? stood ?] in the attic. We couldn't face her, because she would know then that we were there. She never knew that we were there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Back to the workshop. Was there a special hiding place that was built for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Not in the workshop. That was in the attic, was the hiding place.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ah. OK. In the workshop was nothing. My husband used to put on an apron, and the hat, like into the face, and was so called the worker. And I [? stood ?] in the toilet.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I prayed that nobody wants to come in to the toilet to go there, because I was standing in there. If somebody came in there, but wasn't too often. Because he usually had something to do, and he made it by himself. He had no workers or anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: So if somebody came, he introduced my husband as his worker. And I [? stood ?] in the toilet. All the facility that we had was the sink in the toilet.

speaker: interviewee
text: He used to bring us food. And we had also a little stove that we could light there to warm it up, or to help ourself. And that's all we had.

speaker: interviewer
text: So after the workshop, then you went back to the attic.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long were you--

speaker: interviewee
text: In the time that we went away, he changed the attic. He took everything apart, and he made a-- a hiding place. It was like an [? innham ?] there, and he made it straight with the wall. And he made believe there were coats in there. So he, being a carpenter, he had it easy to prepare himself. And he made it-- a lot of Dutch people used to put their coats in the attic. So he put it-- make believe like there were coats in the attic. And he make a double bottom on it, so we could creep in and lock the door from behind and-- and put coats on top. So-- and it had like a little opening, so when you shoved it open, the coats came actually out. But later on, we heard that the Germans used to shoot into that. They knew that the people had hiding places. We didn't know at the time. But when the German came in, they used to shoot all around to see if there was nobody in there. But we had no idea from that. We thought by being in the hiding place, we were safe. But thank God, nobody came.

speaker: interviewer
text: How much longer did you stay in that attic?

speaker: interviewee
text: We [? stood ?] in the attic till the last couple of months from the war. Then we had to go out. There was no food. There was nothing that we can get. So we had no choice. They came, the underground, gave us forged paper, and said, if you have no choice, you have to get out. So my husband went out. He rented an apartment, and we went there. I never was on the street after that no more. I was in that apartment, and I tried to keep away from the window.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there was no-- across the street, there was no-- in the back, there was no-- no family living. It was like a wall. So I [? stood ?] mostly in that room, so nobody could see me. So my husband used to go out and try to get food, but I never went out. Being that I look too much Jewish, I was scared to walk out. And how were you able to come by getting this apartment?

speaker: interviewee
text: I never went away from the apartment. I always lived there.

speaker: interviewer
text: In-- in the--

speaker: interviewee
text: In that apartment, I never went on the street.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, no, I'm saying, the apartment that you went into after the attic--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, that was the apartment.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was your apartment?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was it that you were able to get that apartment, being-- being two Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a lot-- was some vacancy. It was in the Jewish district.

speaker: interviewer
text: But how--

speaker: interviewee
text: A lot of the people had left, and some of the apartments was empty. Underneath lived the landlord, and my husband went in and told him that he comes from the other town. That's what the underground told us. And show him the papers, and the man didn't ask too much questions. And he said, so long as you have the papers from the German, and it's legal papers, I'll rent you the apartment. And we got from the underground the papers that we came from other town, that we were bombed out of town, and we rented the apartment. We had no furniture. But we had the excuse that we were bombed and we had nothing. But Jan and Lucie gave us a little bit pots and pans, and she gave us a little bit-- I don't-- we didn't have no beds, we slept on the floor. But she gave us a, I think, a blanket or so, so we could survive. And that's all we had.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name that was-- that you used on your papers? [? Hoitman, ?] I think was the name that we had. We have a-- a-- a paper that we rented the-- the apartment under the name [? Hoitman, ?] because that was the name that the underground gave us. Do you remember the first name you had?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name? I think E, I don't know. I think Emily or something like that. I'm not sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: Emily [? Hoitman? ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: I think something like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll look at it.

speaker: interviewee
text: I studied it and studied it, so I should remember at the time. And I had to always sort of practice how to write it, the signature.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever do anything else to maintain your-- to maintain or to-- or to prove, to be non-Jewish? Even though you stayed in the apartment--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I never went on the street. I never faced anybody. I never talked with anybody. Not even the landlord. I never talked with him. I never came to the window. I never talked with anybody. I saw them if they were in the back yard. I could see them, but I tried to keep away that they shouldn't see me. That they shouldn't be suspicious or see me. If they know, I don't know. They said after the war that they knew we were Jewish, but I don't know. I have no idea. I tried to keep away from them. I never opened the door for anybody. I never had any contact with anybody. I had my neighbor upstairs, but in the war time, I never contact him, I never talked with them, I never came to the door. I tried to keep away from everybody. After the war, we became friends. It was a young couple, and we became friends. But in the war, nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this was in--

speaker: interviewee
text: 1944. In the beginning-- no, '45. In the beginning of '45. We were liberated in May 5.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who liberated you?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Canadians. Most of the Canadians came in. And some of them was also from Israel, from Palestine, troops, the Jewish brigade, and some American. Mostly Canadians came. And we-- after the war, we tried to go out and find some Jews. Under the Canadian, we tried to find somebody who could understand us, who could talk with us and know what we went through. We tried to find Jewish Canadians, and sometimes we were lucky and had some. And they gave us food. And they-- we told them that we were the survivors. They knew already what was going on, and many times they gave us food, because we didn't have nothing. In the war time, we had to change in order to get food. I had some clothing that we gave away, or we had other things, leather, that my husband changed with another shoe maker. And he gave us coals so we could stoke the furnace with that, first of all for the cold, and then to cook something on it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What we were you able to get to eat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Whatever that we can change. It was-- it didn't make any difference. Whatever we could get. We didn't have no rations, because we were not legal. We had very little food. Not only the Jewish, also the Christian people had no food. But we didn't have any, because we had no coupons. Sometimes the underground gave them some coupons, but we didn't have any, because we were not legal registered in order to get it.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did that go on for, with trading and all that kind of stuff, for food and?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was the whole time in the war. You could only trade for something, because nobody wanted the money. The money wasn't worth nothing. So a lot of non-Jews went to the farmers. And the farmer became very independent. He wanted gold, he wanted clothing, he wanted things that he didn't have. And for that he gave his commodity. He wouldn't want it to give it for the guilder, who wasn't worth nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this-- this continued for how long after--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, the whole time. The whole time in the war time.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about how long after you were liberated?

speaker: interviewee
text: So after the liberation, in the beginning, there was no food to get. I remember once there came the Americans and threw some food on the place, that they're marked. But the German took at lot away.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was in the war time. The German took a lot of that away, and we got very little. You know? So that what is in. After the war, they started bringing in more and more food. Tell me about the events that followed after the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: What kind of events?

speaker: interviewer
text: For you and your husband, after you were liberated.

speaker: interviewee
text: I wrote right away a letter to my sister, who was in the United States. I know she lived in New York. I wrote a letter to my sister. And I-- and I can show you, I got a telegram back that she was happy that I survived with my husband, and that she was married. I knew that she was married to Rein already, and that she had the baby. That was the first knowledge that she had that I was survive. I was the only one in the family who survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you got the telegram, and then were you and your husband--

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I knew that she was there, and she contacted me. She had my address, and she wrote me letters. And she started sending me, also, packages from the United States, so I should have something to eat. That was in '45. She sended me packages, and clothing, and all kind. Most of the clothing, I couldn't wear, because she didn't know what I needed or whatever. But I gave it away, or I changed it for something else.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you try to get some kind of work after you and your husband--

speaker: interviewee
text: In the beginning, I-- we didn't work. What we did, we changed with the Canadians. We went out, we tried to get food. The main thing was food, because that was the thing that was the most critical. And in order to get that, we had to-- my husband used to buy from them [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] cigarettes, giving to Dutch people, and then we tried to give them what they wanted, like watches, cameras, if we had, and we changed that for-- for the cigarettes. And with the cigarettes, we could get. That was the commodity, the cigarettes. People wanted American cigarettes. Because all the years, you couldn't get no cigarettes, and people still wanted to smoke. So we tried with that to get food.

speaker: interviewer
text: When were you able to come to the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think we came in 19-- Which year was it? Which year we came. I don't remember. 1949, I think, we came to the United States. I had to have papers. My sister sent me the papers, affidavits. But it didn't go so fast. My husband was born in Poland, and that was on the quota, so we had to wait till our number come up. But after the war, there were not too many Jews that survived. So when the American Consul wrote us a letter that we can come, we had to-- we had papers that my sister guaranteed that we wouldn't be no burden to the United States, that she would provide for us. And that's how we came.

speaker: interviewer
text: I understand you had a son in between that period of time, too.

speaker: interviewee
text: I had a son, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me-- tell me who he is and when he was born.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was born in September 16, 1948. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I had a very difficult giving birth. I was six weeks in the hospital. My son was born with the instrument, and he was paralyzed. In the-- they [? called ?] [? him-- ?] in the brains, so we had to stay in the hospital. At the time there came the paper from the United States, and I couldn't walk. But we made it. We went to the Consul with the baby. He was only six weeks old when we went to Rotterdam, where the Consul was. And he was a beautiful baby. Everybody carried him around, the consul, and the workers and everything, carried him around. And we got the permit to come. But at the time, it was very hard to come. We couldn't get a ship. Then finally, we got a ship. It was a freight, half freight and half passenger, very little passenger, and that's where we came.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you say your son's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Barry. B-A-R-R-Y. Barry Seeman. But he was fine, he grew up, everything was OK. And he's a wonderful son. We have a lot of pleasure from him.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you arrived in America in 1949.

speaker: interviewee
text: '49, in New York.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what month?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it was February. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We had a difficult time coming. We had-- the sea was very rough. We came to the United States. We went over Bermuda. We had to throw the anchors out and stay in Bermuda, because they couldn't continue. It was a rough time to go over the sea. We came to-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I think it was in February we came to the United States, with a little baby. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I couldn't go to work. My baby was very small. My husband worked with my brother-in-law. We all lived together in one apartment.

speaker: interviewer
text: With your sister and brother-in-law?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Then later on, after three months, we went and we rented out own apartment on the Lower East Side.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about where you went from there. You were living on the Lower East Side--

speaker: interviewee
text: Very difficult to live there. We lived by other people. We didn't have our own apartment. The people were very nice to us. We lived with them for quite a time. Then, a friend for me, that I met there, came in and told me that he had a friend who got an apartment in Brooklyn, but they couldn't make it because it was on the third floor. I said, I'll take anything, so long as I can get mine apartment. And we got-- went to Brooklyn, and we rented that apartment. And that was in Bensonhurst, on 65th Street.
